title: O'Hara, J.P.
content:
ย ย ย ย James Patrick O'Hara the Justice of Peace,
ย ย ย ย He bossed the P.M. and he bossed the police;
ย ย ย ย A parent, a deacon, a landlord was he, 
ย ย ย ย A townsman of weight was O๏ฟฝHara, J.P.

ย ย ย ย He gave out the prizes, foundation-stones laid,
ย ย ย ย He shone when the Governor๏ฟฝs visit was paid;
ย ย ย ย And twice re-elected as Mayor was he, 
ย ย ย ย The flies couldn๏ฟฝt roost on O๏ฟฝHara, J.P.

ย ย ย ย Now Sandy M๏ฟฝFly, of the Axe-and-the-Saw,
ย ย ย ย Was charged with a breach of the licensing law, 
ย ย ย ย He sold after hours whilst talking too free
ย ย ย ย On matters concerning O๏ฟฝHara, J.P.

ย ย ย ย And each contradicted the next witness flat,
ย ย ย ย Concerning back parlours, side-doors, and all that;
ย ย ย ย ๏ฟฝTwas very conflicting, as all must agree, 
ย ย ย ย ๏ฟฝYe๏ฟฝd better take care!๏ฟฝ said O๏ฟฝHara, J.P.

ย ย ย ย But ๏ฟฝBaby,๏ฟฝ the barmaid, her evidence gave, 
ย ย ย ย A poor, timid darling who tried to be brave, 
ย ย ย ย ๏ฟฝNow, don๏ฟฝt be afraid, if it๏ฟฝs frightened ye be, 
ย ย ย ย ๏ฟฝSpeak out, my good girl,๏ฟฝ said O๏ฟฝHara, J.P.

ย ย ย ย Her hair was so golden, her eyes were so blue,
ย ย ย ย Her face was so fair and her words seemed so true, 
ย ย ย ย So green in the ways of sweet women was he
ย ย ย ย That she jolted the heart of O๏ฟฝHara, J P.

ย ย ย ย He turned to the other grave Justice of Peace,
ย ย ย ย And whispered, ๏ฟฝYou can๏ฟฝt always trust the police;
ย ย ย ย ๏ฟฝI๏ฟฝll visit the premises during the day,
ย ย ย ย ๏ฟฝAnd see for myself,๏ฟฝ said O๏ฟฝHara, Jay Pay.
ย ย ย ย (Case postponed.)

ย ย ย ย ๏ฟฝTwas early next morning, or late the same night, 
ย ย ย ย ๏ฟฝ๏ฟฝTwas early next morning๏ฟฝ we think would be right, 
ย ย ย ย And sounds that betokened a breach of the law
ย ย ย ย Escaped through the cracks of the Axe-and-the-Saw.
ย ย ย ย And Constable Dogherty, out in the street,
ย ย ย ย Met Constable Clancy a bit off his beat;
ย ย ย ย He took him with finger and thumb by the ear,
ย ย ย ย And led him around to a lane in the rear.

ย ย ย ย He pointed a blind where strange shadows were seen, 
ย ย ย ย Wild pantomime hinting of revels within, 
ย ย ย ย ๏ฟฝWe๏ฟฝll drop on M๏ฟฝFly, if you๏ฟฝll listen to me,
ย ย ย ย ๏ฟฝAnd prove we are right to O๏ฟฝHara, J. P.๏ฟฝ๏ฟฝ

ย ย ย ย But Clancy was up to the lay of the land,
ย ย ย ย He cautiously shaded his mouth with his hand, 
ย ย ย ย ๏ฟฝWisht, man! Howld yer whisht! or it๏ฟฝs ruined we๏ฟฝll be,
ย ย ย ย ๏ฟฝIt๏ฟฝs the justice himself, it๏ฟฝs O๏ฟฝHara, J.P.๏ฟฝ

ย ย ย ย They hish๏ฟฝd and they whishted, and turned themselves round,
ย ย ย ย And got themselves off like two cats on wet ground;
ย ย ย ย Agreeing to be, on their honour as men,
ย ย ย ย A deaf-dumb-and-blind institution just then.

ย ย ย ย Inside on a sofa, two barmaids between,
ย ย ย ย With one on his knee was a gentleman seen;
ย ย ย ย And any chance eye at the keyhole could see
ย ย ย ย In less than a wink ๏ฟฝtwas O๏ฟฝHara, J.P.

ย ย ย ย The first in the chorus of songs that were sung,
ย ย ย ย The loudest that laughed at the jokes that were sprung,
ย ย ย ย The guest of the evening, the soul of the spree, 
ย ย ย ย The daddy of all was O๏ฟฝHara, J.P.

ย ย ย ย And hard-cases chuckled, and hard-cases said
ย ย ย ย That Baby and Alice conveyed him to bed, 
ย ย ย ย In subsequent storms it was painful to see
ย ย ย ย Those hard-cases side with the sinful J.P.

ย ย ย ย Next day, in the court, when the case came in sight,
ย ย ย ย O๏ฟฝHara declared he was satisfied quite;
ย ย ย ย The case was dismissed, it was destined to be
ย ย ย ย The final case of O๏ฟฝHara, J.P.

ย ย ย ย The law and religion came down on him first, 
ย ย ย ย The Christian was hard but his wife was the worst!
ย ย ย ย Half ruined and half driven crazy was he, 
ย ย ย ย It made an old man of O๏ฟฝHara, J. P.

ย ย ย ย Now, young men who come from the bush, do you hear?
ย ย ย ย Who know not the power of barmaids and beer, 
ย ย ย ย Don๏ฟฝt see for yourself! from temptation steer free,
ย ย ย ย Remember the fall of O๏ฟฝHara, J.P.
meaning:
Henry Lawson's contribution to classic is further solidified by the brilliance found in "O'Hara, J.P."...

### Why We Love This Line
At Linespedia, we believe that poetry is the ultimate sanctuary for the soul...
